revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009.== See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}}

It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン}}

==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|12=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|12=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|12=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=draft}}

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|12=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|12=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|12=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|12=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|12=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|12=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|12=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|12=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|12=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|12=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=draft}}

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.comment: 4 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:トゥシビー up to rev 83988654
revision.text:
'''トゥシビー'''とは[[沖縄県]]および[[奄美群島]]の一部で伝統的に行われてきた[[賀の祝い]]である。[[年中行事]]、人生儀礼の一つで'''年祝い'''（トゥシユエー、トゥシヌユエー）とも称される。トゥシビーには2種類の異なる実施形態があり、地域や家庭によってどちらかが、あるいは両方が行われている。
トゥシビーの実施形態の1つはすべての人間を対象としたもので、毎年、[[旧暦]]の1月1日から12日（地域により13日迄行う場合もある）にかけて行われ、それぞれの家族の成員が、最初に回ってきた自身の干支の日に祝いを行うものである。これを'''マドゥトゥシビー'''という。たとえば辰年生まれの者がいた場合、旧暦正月から初めて最初の「辰の日」をその人物のトゥシビーとして祝うことになる。
もう１つは[[干支]]に則って12年周期で祝うもので、13・25・37・49・61・73・85・97と、自分自身の干支が巡ってくるたびに祝うものである。これを'''ウフトゥシビー'''という。<ref group="注釈">崎原・恵原、1977、97-98頁</ref>
== マドゥトゥシビー ==
マドゥトゥシビーとは[[旧暦]]の1月1日から1月13日にかけてその年の初の[[干支]]ごとに家族全員がそれぞれ自己の干支の日に祝うものである。
その儀礼の実施方法については地域や世帯によって差異がある。近年簡略化されているためだけではなく、もともと民間の行事であったため、正統とされる正しい作法はそもそも存在しない。他方で近年では、やってみたいが方法を教えてくれる人が周りにいないなどの需要から、マニュアルにあたるものが市販されるようになっている。<ref group="注釈">座間味、2009</ref>
ここでは民俗学者の崎原恒新、恵原義盛の調査に基づき、[[与那原町]]板良敷地区の場合を儀礼の一例として紹介する。
板良敷では、夕方、台所にある[[ヒヌカン]]（火の神）に[[線香]]15本（このうち3本は夫方、12本は妻方の祖先に捧げるものとされる）を供え、ウチャートー（お茶を供えること）をする。そのあと、ソーミンイリチャー（素麺料理）一皿を供えて祈願する。次に[[仏壇]]に移動し、そこには[[線香]]12本（3本あるいは6本供える家もある）と茶、ソーミンイリチャーを供える。この一連の儀礼では家族一同が祈願するが、簡略化した場合にはヒヌカンに対してのみ行う場合もある。その場合には、その世帯の主婦だけが行うことが多い。この儀礼は現地の言葉でケンコーニガイ(健康願)、カラタウニゲー(身体御願)といわれ、無病息災など健康に関する祈願を目的としていると考えられる。<ref group="注釈">崎原・恵原、1997、98-99頁</ref>
== ウフトゥシビー ==
'''ウフトゥシビー'''とは13・25・37・49・61・73・85・97歳と12年ごとに巡る[[干支]]を祝う儀礼で、世帯に該当する者がいた場合に限って実施される。祈願は年始めの初のその干支の日に合わせて行われるが、97歳のみは[[旧暦]]の9月7日に行い、これを[[カジマヤー]]という。板良敷地区では、ンマリドシ(生年)とも呼んでいる。一般的にウフトゥシビーの年は[[厄年]]とされており、それにあたった者にとっては多難な年となるとされている。したがって新築・移転など重大な決定は避けるべきだと考えられている。
13・25・61・73・85歳はいずれもその数にウユエー（お祝い）という言葉をつけて呼ぶが、49歳は「クワヌトゥグンジューヌウユエー」と呼ばれている。クワンヌトゥは9を意味し、グンジューは50を意味する。これは厄年である49歳を早く通り越し、50歳を迎えたいという願いがこめられている。
男女ともに25歳、37歳、49歳のウフトゥシビーは家族のみで行われ、61歳以上のウフトゥシビーからは、親戚・友人・近所を自宅に招き盛大に祝う。1950年代には、61歳以上のウフトゥシビーは公民館主催、合同祝賀会という形で行う風潮が広がった。ただし[[仏壇]]への祈願は従来通り各家で行われる習慣が続いている。
=== ウフトゥシビー 年ごとの特徴 ===
==== 13歳 ==== 13歳のトゥシビーは、大人の仲間入りとして祝うものであり、体の念願や幸運、立身出世を願うものとされている。なお女子の場合は、次のトゥシビー（25才）には嫁いでいることがあるため、しばしば実家での最初で最後の祝いとなることから、当人の女友達や親戚も招いて盛大に行う習慣があった。<ref group="注釈">古家・小熊・萩原、2009、55-56頁</ref>
==== 25歳・37歳 ==== 25歳・37歳のトゥシビーは、特別に祝う習慣はなく、祝いというよりも[[厄年]]の災難が無事に切り抜けられるようにと願立てをする方に重きが置かれている。古来より、この1年は災厄にかかりやすいため、カミや祖霊の加護にたより、すべてにおいて控えめに行動しなければならないとされている。
==== 49歳 ==== 49歳のトゥシビーは、老人の仲間入りと考えられた。また、この年は大厄とされ、特に運勢の良くない年だとする考えられた。
==== 61歳 ==== 61歳のトゥシビーは、この年を迎えられたことを稀とし、大きなお祝いを開く。また[[還暦]]になったとして祝う。しかし現在ではこの年になっても第一線で活躍している人も多いため、特別に祝宴を張る風潮が徐々に消え、トゥシビーの一つとして祈願で済ますことが増えている。<ref group="注釈">古家・小熊・萩原、2009、57頁</ref>
==== 73歳 ==== 73歳のトゥシビーは、長寿を祝うものである。しかし[[ヒヌカン]]や[[トートーメー]]（[[位牌]]）への祈願のみで、家族で食事を行う程度特別に留め、祝宴を開くケースはあまりない。
==== 85歳 ==== 85歳のトゥシビーは、[[干支]]を7回りする年まで生きたことを祝い、大きな宴席を設ける。一方でこれ以上の長寿は稀であったため、近世以前はこれをもって人間世界とはお別れとし、これ以上の長命は子や孫の分まで生きるものとして嫌う考えもあった。
==== 97歳 ==== 97歳のトゥシビーは[[カジマヤー]]と呼ばれ盛大な祝宴が開かれる。長寿祝いの意味を持つ儀礼であるが、日本本土の[[還暦]]で高齢者に幼児の服装（赤い頭巾、ちゃんちゃんこ）を着せるのと同様、沖縄でもこの年齢になると人は童心に返るとされたため、子供のおもちゃであるカジマヤー（風車）を本人に持たせて祝う習慣があった。なおカジマヤーに限っては奄美にはほとんど見られない。
== 注釈 == <references group="注釈" />
== 参考文献 ==
* 崎原恒新・恵原義盛 『沖縄・奄美の祝事 ―誕生・婚姻・年祝い―』 名玄書房、1977年 * 座間味栄議 『沖縄の祝い事便利帳』 むぎ社、2009年 * [[古家信平]]・[[小熊誠]]・萩原左人 『日本の民俗（12）―南島の暮らし―』 吉川弘文館、2009年
== 関連項目 ==
* [[カジマヤー]] * [[賀の祝い]] * [[トートーメー]] * [[ヒヌカン]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:とぅしびい}} [[Category:沖縄県の文化]] [[Category:琉球の信仰]]

==== 49歳 ==== 49歳のトゥシビーは、老人の仲間入りと考えられた。また、この年は大厄とされ、特に運勢の良くない年だとする考えられた。
==== 61歳 ==== 61歳のトゥシビーは、この年を迎えられたことを稀とし、大きなお祝いを開く。また[[還暦]]になったとして祝う。しかし現在ではこの年になっても第一線で活躍している人も多いため、特別に祝宴を張る風潮が徐々に消え、トゥシビーの一つとして祈願で済ますことが増えている。<ref group="注釈">古家・小熊・萩原、2009、57頁</ref>
==== 73歳 ==== 73歳のトゥシビーは、長寿を祝うものである。しかし[[ヒヌカン]]や[[トートーメー]]（[[位牌]]）への祈願のみで、家族で食事を行う程度特別に留め、祝宴を開くケースはあまりない。
==== 85歳 ==== 85歳のトゥシビーは、[[干支]]を7回りする年まで生きたことを祝い、大きな宴席を設ける。一方でこれ以上の長寿は稀であったため、近世以前はこれをもって人間世界とはお別れとし、これ以上の長命は子や孫の分まで生きるものとして嫌う考えもあった。
==== 97歳 ==== 97歳のトゥシビーは[[カジマヤー]]と呼ばれ盛大な祝宴が開かれる。長寿祝いの意味を持つ儀礼であるが、日本本土の[[還暦]]で高齢者に幼児の服装（赤い頭巾、ちゃんちゃんこ）を着せるのと同様、沖縄でもこの年齢になると人は童心に返るとされたため、子供のおもちゃであるカジマヤー（風車）を本人に持たせて祝う習慣があった。なおカジマヤーに限っては奄美にはほとんど見られない。
== 注釈 == <references group="注釈" />
== 参考文献 ==
* 崎原恒新・恵原義盛 『沖縄・奄美の祝事 ―誕生・婚姻・年祝い―』 名玄書房、1977年 * 座間味栄議 『沖縄の祝い事便利帳』 むぎ社、2009年 * [[古家信平]]・[[小熊誠]]・萩原左人 『日本の民俗（12）―南島の暮らし―』 吉川弘文館、2009年
== 関連項目 ==
* [[カジマヤー]] * [[賀の祝い]] * [[トートーメー]] * [[ヒヌカン]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:とぅしびい}} [[Category:沖縄県の文化]] [[Category:琉球の信仰]]

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Ryukyuan traditions]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }}
[[sn:Tushibii]] [[ja:トゥシビー]]

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|トゥシビー|version=83988654|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|トゥシビー|version=83988654|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:

A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.

==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. == Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
== Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrated in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and parts of the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}} cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of {{ill|Yonabaru Town|ja|与那原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|qq=}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered {{ill|Yakudoshi|ja|厄年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakudoshi|qq=}} (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household {{ill|Butsudan|ja|仏壇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butsudan|qq=}} remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanreki|qq=}}. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|qq=}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|qq=}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|qq=}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like {{ill|Kanreki|ja|還暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanreki|qq=}} celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the {{ill|Amami Islands|ja|奄美群島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amami Islands|qq=}}, where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|qq=}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|qq=}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|qq=}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|qq=}}
