revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.

== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}
{{translated page|ja|トゥシビー|version=83988654|comment=Imported full ja history}}

A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}
{{translated page|ja|トゥシビー|version=83988654|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:トゥシビー]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q109360194]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Tushibii == '''Tushibii''' is a traditional form of {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} celebrated in [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] and parts of the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]]. It is considered a type of {{ill|Annual event|ja|年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual event|qq=}} and life ritual, also known as '''Toshi-iwai''' (トゥシユエー, トゥシヌユエー). There are two distinct forms of Tushibii, with families and regions practicing either one or both traditions.
One type of Tushibii applies to all individuals and is observed annually between the first and twelfth days of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}'s first month (some regions extend it to the thirteenth day). Each family member celebrates on the first occurrence of their [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] day during this period. This practice is known as '''Madu Tushibii'''. For example, if someone was born in the Year of the Dragon, they would celebrate on the first "Dragon Day" that occurs after the Lunar New Year.
The second type follows the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Chinese zodiac]] cycle, with celebrations occurring every twelve years at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97. This is called '''Ufu Tushibii'''.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1977, pp. 97-98</ref>
== Madu Tushibii ==
'''Madu Tushibii''' is celebrated annually from the first to the thirteenth day of the {{ill|Lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, where each family member observes their first zodiac day of the year.
The method of performing the ritual varies by region and household. Not only has it been simplified in recent times, but because it originated as a folk tradition, there is no single "correct" method. However, due to modern interest in reviving the tradition, instructional manuals are now commercially available.<ref group="note">Zamami, 2009</ref>
Based on field studies by folklorists {{ill|Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja|崎原恒新|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakihara Tsuneshin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ehara Yoshimori|ja|恵原義盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehara Yoshimori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ritual as practiced in the Itarashiki district of [[:en:Yonabaru, Okinawa|Yonabaru Town]] is as follows:
In the evening, offerings are made to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} (fire deity) in the kitchen. This includes 15 {{ill|incense sticks|ja|線香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/incense sticks|1=Joss stick|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q842366}}—three for the husband's ancestors and twelve for the wife's ancestors—along with tea offerings. A plate of {{ill|sōmen champurū|ja|ソーミンイリチャー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōmen champurū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (stir-fried noodles) is then presented, and prayers are made. The ritual then moves to the [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] (Buddhist altar), where twelve incense sticks (some families use three or six) are offered along with tea and sōmen champurū. Traditionally, the whole family participates in these prayers, but in simplified versions, only the Hinukan ceremony is performed, often solely by the household matriarch. This ritual, known locally as '''Kenko Nigai''' (健康願) or '''Karatau Nigee''' (身体御願), is primarily intended to pray for health and well-being.<ref group="note">Sakihara & Ehara, 1997, pp. 98-99</ref>
== Ufu Tushibii ==
'''Ufu Tushibii''' is a ritual observed at ages 13, 25, 37, 49, 61, 73, 85, and 97, celebrating the return of one's birth zodiac sign in the 12-year cycle. The ritual is performed at the beginning of the year on the first occurrence of the person's zodiac day, except for age 97, which is celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month and called {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}. In Itarashiki, this ritual is also called '''Nmaridoshi''' (ンマリドシ). Traditionally, Ufu Tushibii years were considered [[:en:Yakudoshi|Yakudoshi]] (unlucky years), so those reaching these ages were expected to avoid major life changes such as moving or building a new house.
Ages 13, 25, 61, 73, and 85 are called "Uyuee" (お祝い) years, while age 49 is called "Kuwan Tutugunjuu no Uyuee." The term "Kuwan Tutugunjuu" combines "kuwan" (9) and "gunjuu" (50), symbolizing the wish to safely pass through age 49, which was seen as a critical yakudoshi year.
For ages 25, 37, and 49, the celebration is limited to immediate family. From age 61 onward, extended family, friends, and neighbors are invited, with grand celebrations often held at home. By the 1950s, community centers began organizing large-scale Ufu Tushibii gatherings for those aged 61 and older. However, prayers at the household [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] remained an essential part of the tradition.
=== Ufu Tushibii by Age ===
==== Age 13 ==== At age 13, Tushibii marks the transition into adulthood and is meant to ensure good fortune, health, and success. For girls, this may be their last celebration at their parental home before marriage, so it was traditionally observed with a grand gathering of female friends and relatives.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, pp. 55-56</ref>
==== Ages 25 & 37 ==== These ages do not involve elaborate celebrations. Instead, the focus is on prayers for protection from misfortune, as these are considered yakudoshi years. Traditionally, people were advised to act cautiously and rely on the blessings of deities and ancestral spirits.
==== Age 49 ==== Age 49 marks entry into elderhood. It is considered a major yakudoshi year and is believed to be particularly inauspicious.
==== Age 61 ==== Age 61 is a major celebration, marking a rare milestone and often including a large banquet. It is also known as [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]]. However, as many people remain active in their careers at this age today, extravagant feasts have become less common, with some opting for a simple prayer ceremony instead.<ref group="note">Furuya, Oguma, & Hagiwara, 2009, p. 57</ref>
==== Age 73 ==== This Tushibii is considered a longevity celebration. It is usually observed with a simple meal and prayers to the {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}} and {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} (family ancestral tablets), rather than a formal banquet.
==== Age 85 ==== At age 85, reaching seven full zodiac cycles is celebrated with a large banquet. In past centuries, people believed living beyond this age meant extending life beyond one's natural allotment, sometimes leading to reluctance to celebrate further longevity.
==== Age 97 ==== Known as {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}}, this celebration includes a grand banquet. Like [[:en:Sixtieth birthday in the Sinosphere|Kanreki]] celebrations on the Japanese mainland, which feature red clothing symbolizing a return to infancy, Okinawan tradition involves presenting the celebrant with a pinwheel, symbolizing a return to childlike joy. Kajimaya is rare in the [[:en:Amami Islands|Amami Islands]], where the custom is not widely observed.
== Notes == <references group="note" />
== References == * Sakihara Tsuneshin & Ehara Yoshimori, "Okinawa and Amami Celebrations: Birth, Marriage, and Longevity," Meigen Publishing, 1977. * Zamami Eigi, "Okinawa Celebration Guidebook," Mugi Publishing, 2009. * Furuya Nobuhira, Oguma Makoto, & Hagiwara Sato, "Japanese Folklore (12) – Life in the Southern Islands," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009. == See Also == * {{ill|Kajimaya|ja|カジマヤー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajimaya|1=Kajimaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28693017}} * {{ill|Kan-no-iwai|ja|賀の祝い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-no-iwai|1=Kan-no-iwai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28684874}} * {{ill|Tōtōmē|ja|トートーメー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōtōmē|1=Tōtōmē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17220557}} * {{ill|Hinukan|ja|ヒヌカン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hinukan|WD=Q48744091}}
{{translated page|ja|トゥシビー|version=83988654|comment=Imported full ja history}}
